extraction.title: Origin of the Electric Distribution Industry

points.text: Thomas Edison operated the Pearl Street Steam-electric plant and the pioneer distribution (DC) system.
1882: Water-wheel driven generator installed in Appleton, Wisconsin, operating at 2400V DC (59 km travelled).
sub_points: DC had shortcomings because it was hard to deliver over long distances.

points.text: 1885: AC current by Nikola Tesla and George Westinghouse.
sub_points: 4000V AC transmission line between Oregon and Portland.

points.text: Improvements and advancements led to transmission and generation capacity at lower + decreasing unit costs.
sub_points: But simultaneously, concentration and capacities of individual plants also increased investment for large projects.

points.text: We still need to improve these technologies due to our growing demand for electricity.
sub_points: Minimizing land use, water use, and environmental impact.

sections.heading: Developing the National Electric Power Grid

points.text: Transmission lines allow generators to work synchronously — but has issues with it:
sub_points: A generator/transmission line failure in one area does not identically affect those in the same area.

sections.heading: Improvements in the EPS / Golden Age

points.text: (Section begins — notes cut off / continue on next page.)

extraction.summary_topic_line: History and Development of the Electric Power Distribution Industry
extraction.summary_gist: These lecture notes trace the origin of the electric distribution industry from Edison's 1880s DC Pearl Street plant through the 1882 Appleton water-wheel generator and the shift to AC power via Tesla and Westinghouse in 1885. Key themes include DC's limitations over long distances, the advantages of AC transmission (4000V line between Oregon and Portland), and how improvements lowered unit costs while simultaneously requiring larger capital investment. The notes also introduce the National Electric Power Grid, noting that synchronous generator interconnection means faults propagate locally (nearer = more affected), and briefly open the topic of EPS Golden Age improvements.
